coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of dual emission carbon dots for the ratiometric fluorescent detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ke
Wang, Xinyu
Liu, Xiaoyang
Li, Enshou
Zhao, Rusong
Yang, Shenghong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with dual emission property can provide a convenient way to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor, which contributes to accurate determination of the target analytes. In this work, we present a simple and effective route to prepare dual emission CDs (DE-CDs) through the solvothermal reaction of o-phenylenediamine and ethanolamine. Under the excitation of UV light, DE-CDs not only show two distinguishable fluorescent emission peaks (430 nm, 550 nm), but also exhibit good water solubility and ratio fluorescence stability. The synthesized DE-CDs were employed as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the sensitive and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). Under optimal analysis conditions, a good linear relationship was obtained with the TNP concentration range from 0.2 to 50 µM and the limit of detection can reach 50 nM. The proposed method was applied successfully for the TNP detection in real water samples with recoveries of 86.8–116.3%. Furthermore, cell imaging experiments confirm the feasibility of visualizing intracellular TNP in live HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual emission
Ratiometric fluorescent
TNP detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TNP in water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of DE-CDs and its application in the ratiometric fluorescent detection of TNP.
Fluorescence spectra of o-CDs (a) and DE-CDs (b) with excitation wavelength from 355 to 380 nm.
TEM images and the size distribution of DE-CDs (a) and HRTEM images of DE-CDs (b), XRD pattern of DE-CDs (c) and FTIR spectra of o-CDs and DE-CDs (d).
XPS survey scan of DE-CDs (a) and high-resolution C 1 s (b), N 1 s (c) and O 1 s spectra of DE-CDs (d).

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of DE-CDs (a), fluorescence spectra of DE-CDs before and after the addition of TNP (DE-CDs dispersed in buffer solution with pH=4, excitation wavelength at 365 nm) (b), UV–Vis absorption spectra (solid line) of DE-CDs, TNP and DE-CDs-TNP complex and fluorescence spectrum (dashed line) of DE-CDs (c).

The fluorescence spectra of the DE-CDs in presence of different concentrations of TNP (a), the plot of I550/I430 values versus the concentrations of TNP (b), the fluorescence spectra of DE-CDs in the rang of 380–480 nm and 500–650 nm under the excitation of 365 nm (c) and 460 nm (d), respectively; the plot of I550/I430 values versus the concentrations of TNP in the range from 0 to 50 µM (e) (I430 value is obtained by 365 nm excitation, I550 value is obtained by 460 nm excitation).
The selectivity of ratiometric fluorescence sensor for TNP detection toward various organics (a) and common inorganic ions (b). (TNP: 30 µM, other organics: 30 µM, inorganic ions: 30 µM).
Fluorescent microscopy images (a-c) of Hela cells. Cells were unstained (a), cells were treated with DE-CDs (b), cells were incubated with DE-CDs and then incubated with TNP (c).
Determination of spiked TNP in tap water, pond water and spring water samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of dual emission carbon dots for the ratiometric fluorescent detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol and cell imaging

Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with dual emission property can provide a convenient way to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor, which contributes to accurate determination of the target analytes. In this work, we present a simple and effective route to prepare dual emission CDs (DE-CDs) through the solvothermal reaction of o-phenylenediamine and ethanolamine. Under the excitation of UV light, DE-CDs not only show two distinguishable fluorescent emission peaks (430 nm, 550 nm), but also exhibit good water solubility and ratio fluorescence stability. The synthesized DE-CDs were employed as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the sensitive and selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). Under optimal analysis conditions, a good linear relationship was obtained with the TNP concentration range from 0.2 to 50 µM and the limit of detection can reach 50 nM. The proposed method was applied successfully for the TNP detection in real water samples with recoveries of 86.8–116.3%. Furthermore, cell imaging experiments confirm the feasibility of visualizing intracellular TNP in live HeLa cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroaromatic explosives have been used extensively in military industry, firework manufacture, daily life and aviation over the years. Due to their water-soluble characteristic, nitroaromatic explosives can gradually accumulate in the soil, water, and other natural environments in the process of production, storage and use, and has become a hidden danger to human life [1,2]. Among them, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) is a typical and commonly used nitroaromatic explosive [3–5]. Compared with the later 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), TNP is more unstable and has more explosion risk [6]. The long-term use of TNP has resulted in a large amount of residue in soil and water, which is difficult to be mineralized due to its strong electron-withdrawing ability [7,8]. TNP and its metabolites can not only cause series of environmental problems but also lead to many negative effects on human body, such as chronic poisoning, hematopoietic system injury, cancer, etc. [9]. The permissible level of TNP allowed in drinking surface water sources cannot exceed 0.5 mg·L−1 according to environmental quality standards for surface water of China [10]. Therefore, the detection of TNP in environmental and biological medium has become particularly important and has always been the focus of research [11].

Conventional TNP detection methods mainly include high-performance liquid chromatography [12,13], infrared spectroscopy and surface-enhanced Raman scattering [14,15], electrochemical method [16], mass spectrometry method [17], and so on. However, these methods are limited by expensive instruments and tedious or cumbersome operation procedures. Compared to traditional analytical methods, the fluorescent method for TNP detection has attracted much attention in recent years due to its high sensitivity, fast response, and easy operation [18,19]. Up to now, various fluorescent materials have been developed as effective sensors for the detection of TNP, such as conjugated polymers [20], organic small molecules [21], metal nanoclusters [22], metal organic frameworks [23], and semiconductor quantum dots [24], etc. However, most of these fluorescent materials are either intrinsically toxic or difficult to degrade in natural environment due to their components of heavy metal ions or highly conjugated skeletons, both of which may cause secondary pollution [25]. The environmental risks of the above materials sparked an intense interest in the development of high-security fluorescent nanomaterials for the TNP detection.

Compared to nanophosphors, pyridine and other optical materials in TNP detection [26,27], carbon dots (CDs) have become a good alternative due to good water solubility, light stability, high biocompatibility, and easy to be synthesized as an emerging fluorescent material [28,29]. Gao et al. reported a (3‑chloro-2-hydroxypropyl) trimethylammonium chloride modified blue fluorescent CDs for TNP detection through the molecular interaction of TNP and CDs [30]. Wang et al. prepared one kind of amphiphilic CDs with blue fluorescence through the reaction of p-phenylenediamine and L-histidine. The as-synthesized CDs were successfully used for TNP detection in various polar and non-polar solvents [31]. Despite great success in TNP detection by CDs, while most of them are triggered by TNP-induced fluorescence quenching with single fluorescent emission response, which is easily affected by the fluctuation of test environment [32]. By contrast, ratiometric fluorescent detection system can provide more accurate measurement results through calculating the intensity ratio of two fluorescent signals, which can resist external interference [33–35]. That is, CDs with ratiometric fluorescent property (dual-emission CDs, DE-CDs) can detect targets by calculating the fluorescence intensity of the two emission peaks, while traditional single-emission CDs can only be applied based on the changes of fluorescence intensity. However, DE-CDs are rarely synthesized at present, and most of the developed ratiometric fluorescent probes are constructed by attaching other fluorescent materials [36,37]. Therefore, it is still significant to develop a reasonable route to synthesize DE-CDs and construct a ratiometric fluorescent detection system to realize more accurate determination of TNP.

In this study, a kind of fluorescent DE-CDs was developed by using o-phenylenediamine and ethanolamine as raw materials through one-step solvothermal reaction. The prepared DE-CDs can emit blue and yellow fluorescence meanwhile under the excitation of UV light as expected, which were successfully applied for the ratiometric fluorescent detection of TNP in high selectivity and sensitivity (Scheme 1 ). Furthermore, the fluorescence signal of the prepared CDs responds to TNP almost completed immediately. Compared with previous works, the advantage of this work is that the detection of TNP by DE-CDs do not require a long wait, which greatly improves the speed and efficiency of determination. Furthermore, the applicability of the established analysis method was evaluated through detecting TNP in real environmental water samples. Significantly, the as-prepared DE-CDs were also successfully used for the visualization of intracellular TNP in live Hela cells.

Ethanolamine was received from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). O-phenylenediamine (o-PD), ethanol, TNP and DNT were obtained from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). TNT was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water with resistance of 18.25 MΩ·cm was used throughout the whole work (UPR-II-40 L, Sichuan, China).

The fluorescence properties of DE-CDs were measured on a F97Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). A UV 2800S spectrophotometer (Shanghai Shunyuhengping technologies Co., LTD, China) was used to characterize the absorption spectra of DE-CDs. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were carried out on a Nexus 670 spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). The morphology and lattice information of the DE-CDs were recorded by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was obtained on X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALABXi+). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed using a D8 ADVANCE03030502 X'Pert diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The particle size distribution of DE-CDs was characterized by a Laser particle size analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Panalytical, Britain). Live-cell fluorescence imaging photos were taken with a Carl Zeiss Microscopy (Germany). The MTT test was carried out on a Multiskan FC Type 357 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Herein, the solvothermal method is used to synthesize DE-CDs. For detailed, 0.3 g of o-phenylenediamine and 1 mL of ethanolamine was sufficiently dissolved in 19 mL of ethanol and then transferred to a 50 mL autoclave made of polytetrafluoroethylene. After that, the sealed carefully autoclave was heated and reacted for 10 h at 220 °C. After the reaction finished, the obtained products were purified by the dialysis process, and freeze drying process. The synthesized DE-CDs was stored in the refrigerator (4 °C) for further use.

Typically, 9 µL of TNP with different concentrations was added to 3 mL of disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid buffer solution (5 mM, pH 4.0) including 0.4 mg·mL−1 DE-CDs. After incubation for 10 s at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of 430 nm and 550 nm was measured with the excitation of 365 nm. The selectivity of the sensor to other nitroaromatic explosives, metal ions, common anions, and some organics, was determined according to traditional procedures, including 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −, p-nitrophenol (NP), o-nitrotoluene, 4-nitrotoluene (NT), phenol (PhOH) and o-dichlorobenzene (DClB), glucose, m-phenylenediamine, benzoic acid, melamine, ascorbic acid. The reliability of the proposed TNP analysis method was verified by spiked water samples analysis and cell imaging experiment.

In this study, we proposed an effective route to prepare DE-CDs through solvent thermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine and ethanolamine. As shown in Fig. 1 a, we can see that the o-CDs synthesized by using single o-phenylenediamine as reactant just can exhibit a sole emission center at 565 nm. However, the synthesized DE-CDs by using o-phenylenediamine and ethanolamine can exhibit obvious unique dual emission characteristic with emission wavelengths at 430 nm and 550 nm (Fig. 1b). The fluorescence quantum yield of DE-CDs is 7.8%. Similar to some previous reports, the dual emission fluorescence of DE-CDs should be attributed to the overall performance of surface state and intrinsic electronic conjugate structure [38,39]. The morphology and size of DE-CDs was characterized by TEM and dynamic light scattering. TEM images are shown in Fig. 2 a,b, DE-CDs is monodisperse with a nearly spherical shape at different magnification. Moreover, in Fig. 2b inset, an obvious lattice structure can be observed through a HRTEM image, and the lattice spacing is about 0.21 nm, which matches with the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [40]. In addition, the size distribution of synthesized DE-CDs is approximately 2.5 nm-4.5 nm (Fig. 2a inset). In Fig. 2c, the XRD pattern of DE-CDs showed only a broad diffraction peak with a center at 23.5°, corresponding to (002) plane of the graphitic carbon. The results show that the obtained DE-CDs after the high temperature and high pressure reaction have been fully carbonized [41]. The FT-IR spectrum of DE-CDs was shown in Fig. 2d, the absorption peaks from 3100 cm−1 to 3400 cm−1 correspond to the vibration bands of NH and OH bond of the DE-CDs [42]. The peaks at 1630 cm−1, 1500 cm−1, 1460 cm−1 can be assigned to stretching vibrations of C=C [43,44]. And we can clearly find the stretching vibrations of C-N at 1270 cm−1 and C-O at 1120 cm−1. Compared to o-CDs, DE-CDs have two new characteristic peaks at about 1590 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1, which matches with C=O and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively [42,43]. Above results indicate that the emergence of the blue fluorescent center comes from the improvement of the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the DE-CDs by ethanolamine.

The XPS survey spectrum of DE-CDs (Fig. 3 a) indicates the existence of C 1 s (284.8 eV), N 1 s (399.0 eV), O 1 s (531.8 eV). From the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s in Fig. 3b, we can see the signals of C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and C=O at 284.7 eV, 285.7 eV and 287.3 eV, respectively [45–47]. In Fig. 3c and d, N 1 s spectrum can be divided into two peaks at 398.8 eV (N-H) and 400.1 eV (C-N-C), and O 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.7 and 532.1 eV, corresponding to C=O and C-O bonds [48,49]. These results are consistent with the FT-IR data, which confirms that DE-CDs possess abundant surface functional groups.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of DE-CDs exhibits three peaks (Fig. 4 a). Peaks at 278 nm and 448 nm can be ascribed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [50]. In addition, the peak at 378 nm could be assigned to the presence of CN of amines [51]. Unlike most previous reported CDs, under the excitation of 365 nm, DE-CDs can emit blue (430 nm) and yellow (550 nm) fluorescence simultaneously (Fig. 4b). And the optimal excitation wavelengths of 430 nm and 550 nm emission bands are at 345 nm and 385 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). The dual emission property can be maintained at a broad pH range of 4–10 (Fig. S2). Based on the dual emission property, DE-CDs can be used to establish a ratiometric fluorescent sensor. Therefore, the stability of the proposed sensor was studied systematically. Moreover, the fluorescent intensity ratio of I550/I430 has no obvious change for 120 min with continuous excitation light illumination (Fig. S3). And the DE-CDs also possess good ion strength tolerance and the fluorescence ratio was maintained even at NaCl solution with high concentration (10 mM, Fig. S4). The results suggest that DE-CDs possess good ratiometric fluorescent stability, which is essential for real sensing application.

The synthesized DE-CDs were employed as an efficient ratiometric fluorescence sensor to achieve the accurate detection of TNP. From Fig. 4b, after adding TNP, the two fluorescence emission peaks of DE-CDs were quenched in different ratios and the quenching rate of blue fluorescence (430 nm) is higher than that of yellow fluorescence (550 nm). The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of DE-CDs by TNP can be explained by the molecular interaction and internal filtration effect. On the one hand, as previous report, the abundant amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of DE-CDs can interact with the phenolic groups of TNP based on electrostatic interaction/hydrogen bonding, and leading the fluorescent quenching of DE-CDs [52]. In this study, as shown in Fig. 4b, after the TNP is added to the DE-CDs aqueous solution, the blue fluorescence peak appeared slightly red shift from 430 nm to 460 nm, which proves the effective electrostatic interaction of DE-CDs and TNP [52]. In addition, the strong electron-withdrawing property of nitro group of TNP also can cause fluorescence quenching as a result of efficient electron transfer from electron-rich DE-CDs to the electron-deficient TNP [53]. Moreover, as shown in Table S1, zeta potential was used to character the stability and the interaction in system (buffer solution with pH=4). It is obviously observed that the zeta potentials of DE-CDs were decrease after adding TNP in system, which prove that the interaction of DE-CDs and TNP in sensing system.

The ratiometric fluorescence quenching mechanism can be concluded from the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. From Fig. 4c, the UV–Vis absorption of TNP is mainly between 300 nm and 450 nm, and there is a significant overlap with the excitation spectrum of DE-CDs, which indicates that fluorescence quenching can be caused by the inner filter effect [54,55]. After adding TNP, the fluorescence of DE-CDs was quenched due to the reduction of the absorbed photons. On the other hand, the emission spectrum of DE-CDs and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of DE-CDs-TNP complex are partially overlapped. Moreover, the degree of overlap with blue fluorescence is greater than that of yellow fluorescence. The blue fluorescence would be quenched easier than the yellow fluorescence, thus, an effective ratiometric fluorescence sensor for TNP detection was established.

The effects of incubation time, temperature and salt concentration on the fluorescence response were first explored. As shown in Fig. S5, after adding TNP, the fluorescence signal response can be stabilized after 10 s. The suitable incubation temperature is 25–30 °C (Fig. S6). From the results in Fig. S7, it can be known that even a higher salt concentration (10 mM) still cannot affect the signal of DE-CDs to TNP. At the same time, in order to avoid the interference of other nitro explosives, pH=4 was selected as the incubation condition, according to a previous report [56]. Under optimal conditions, in disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid buffer solution (5 mM) with pH=4, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm and 550 nm decreased to different degrees with the increase of TNP concentration (Fig. 5 a). In this study, the fluorescence ratio change was described as I550/I430. In Fig. 5b, the ratio values of I550/I430 raise gradually with increasing concentration of TNP, and shows an excellent linear relationship in the range of 0.2–50 µM (R2=0.9964). The limit of detection can reach ∼50 nM based on 3 times of signal to noise. Furthermore, to amplify the signal change, the value of the I430 and the value of I550 also can be obtained by different excitation wavelengths, respectively [57]. As shown in Fig. 5c,d, the intensity at 430 nm gradually decreased with the increase of the TNP concentration (365 nm excitation). However, when the aqueous was excited at 460 nm, the fluorescence intensity of 550 nm varied very weak. The I550/I430 value obtained at different excitation wavelengths was calculated, and good linear regression was obtained in a TNP concentration range of 0.2–50 µM (R2 = 0.9902) (Fig. 5e) with a detection limit at 50 nM (0.011 mg·L−1). The detection limit of proposed method is much lower than the permissible level of TNP allowed in the drinking surface water sources (0.5 mg·L−1) [10], which suggests the practicability of developed analysis method. Compared with the previously reported TNP detection methods using CDs (Table S2), the detection limit of DE-CDs-based sensor possesses an obvious advantage. Therefore, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent method is suitable for the accurate, fast and sensitive detection of TNP.

To evaluate the selectivity of DE-CDs for TNP, the responses of DE-CDs to metal ions, common organic reagents, and organic molecules with similar structures to TNP were examined. Metal ions and common anions can co-exist in natural water samples, including Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −. Common organic reagents used m-phenylenediamine, benzoic acid, glucose, melamine, ascorbic acid and organic molecules with a similar structure to TNP, such as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), p-nitrophenol (NP), o-nitrotoluene, 4-nitrotoluene (NT), phenol (PhOH) and o-dichlorobenzene (DClB). DE-CDs exhibit excellent selectivity, as displayed in Fig. 6 a and b, only Fe3+ can cause slight interference on TNP detection. However, the interference from Fe3+ can be easily eliminated by adding ascorbic acid, which has no effect on the detection of TNP. Hence, the synthesized DE-CDs can be applied for the determination of TNP in a complicated sample system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TNP in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the proposed method was verified through the detection of TNP in real environment water samples, including tap water, pond water and spring water. No TNP was detected in the original samples and TNP was spiked to the original samples with different concentrations. As displayed in Table 1 , the recoveries are between 97.5% and 103.8% and the RSDs are no more than 3.9%. Hence, the proposed method possesses enough reliability and applicability in the TNP detection.

To explore the feasibility of bioimaging of DE-CDs in living cells, MTT test was first performed to evaluate the cell cytotoxicity of DE-CDs by using Hela cell as model cell. Fig. S8 indicated that cell viability can reach 81.4% even if the concentration of DE-CDs reaches 0.5 mg·mL−1. The result suggests that DE-CDs possess excellent cellular compatibility and are suitable for biological system. Therefore, cell imaging experiments were further performed to realize the intracellular imaging of TNP (Fig. 7 a–c). From Fig. 7a we can see that the unstained cells cannot show fluorescence signals. However, after incubating Hela cells with DE-CDs, the cells exhibit obvious yellow-white fluorescence due to the combination of yellow and blue fluorescence (Fig. 7b). Furthermore, after adding TNP, the fluorescence signal of DE-CDs in cells decreased obviously (Fig. 7c). All the above results indicated that the synthesized DE-CDs was membrane permeable and can be applied to visualize the intracellular TNP through cell imaging.

In summary, we successfully synthesized high-efficiency DE-CDs based on the solvothermal reaction of o-phenylenediamine and ethanolamine. The synthesized DE-CDs possess good ratio fluorescence stability and were successfully used as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor to realize the rapid, sensitive and selective determination of TNP. Importantly, the designed ratiometric fluorescent sensor was successfully applied for the analysis of TNP in real samples and the visualization of intracellular TNP in live HeLa cells. The proposed synthesis strategy of DE-CDs might provide a reference for designing new routes to prepare other types of dual-emission fluorescent nanomaterials to satisfy the demands of accurate analysis.

Ke Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis. Xinyu Wang: Validation, Investigation. Xiaoyang Liu: Methodology. Enshou Li: Data curation. Rusong Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shenghong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interestsor personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21806189), the Young Doctor Cooperative Foundation of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (No.2019BSHZ0027), Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (No.ts20190948) and Program for Scientific Research Innovation Team in Colleges and Universities of Shandong Province.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2022.133167.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitroaromatic compounds, from synthesis to biodegradation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection based on the composite material of magnetic covalent organic frameworks, molecularly imprinted polymers and carbon dots
